summary: Gruber follows up on his criticism of Apple's commercial telling iPhone users not to worry about photo storage, arguing that iCloud's free 5 GB tier has been unchanged since Steve Jobs announced it in 2011. He compares Apple and Google's cloud storage pricing, finding them largely comparable at paid tiers but noting Google offers 3x more free storage. He argues that iCloud storage tiers should be periodically adjusted for 'inflation' — accounting for larger device storage, higher resolution photos and video, and growing user expectations. While acknowledging that offering more free storage to over a billion users would be expensive, he contends that if Apple is going to run ads telling users not to worry about photo storage, they should back that up with more generous iCloud offerings.
key_points: iCloud's free 5 GB tier hasn't changed since its 2011 launch, while device storage and media file sizes have grown dramatically
Google offers 3x the free storage (15 GB vs 5 GB), though paid tiers are largely comparable between the two companies
iCloud storage 'inflation' should account for increased device storage (16-64 GB in 2011 vs hundreds of GB today) and video file sizes (65 MB/min for 1080p vs 440 MB/min for 4K 60fps)
The stingy free tier may be why iPhones still default to 1080p video instead of 4K, costing hundreds of millions of users better quality permanent memories
Apple can't credibly run commercials telling users not to worry about photo storage while offering only 5 GB free
money_quotes: The free tier of iCloud has been stuck at 5 GB since the day iCloud was announced, which was so long ago that Steve Jobs announced it at his final WWDC keynote in 2011.
Like the stingy U.S. minimum wage — which was last increased, to $7.25/hour, in 2009 — these tiers ought to be adjusted for 'inflation' periodically, but aren't.

It's very easy for me and you to just declare that Apple ought to just foot the bill to offer more storage for over a billion users worldwide, but we're not the ones making new TV commercials telling iPhone 15 users they needn't worry about photo storage.
If Apple really wants iPhone users not to worry about photo storage, they should offer more with iCloud, cost-to-Apple be damned.
For the masses, iCloud backup is the only backup.
Is the paucity of available iCloud storage in the typical user's account the reason why iPhones still default to shooting 1080p video rather than 4K?
That's a big difference in resolution for permanent memories.
key_insight: Apple's iCloud free tier has been frozen at 5 GB since 2011 while device storage and media file sizes have ballooned, making it hypocritical for Apple to advertise worry-free photo storage without actually providing it.
